# Time-Indexed Regression: From Flexible Models to Trend{#TimeIndexedRegression}

[In class version](https://R-Resources.massey.ac.nz/161251/Lectures/Lecture32.html)

<!--- Data is on
https://r-resources.massey.ac.nz/data/161251/
--->

```{r setup, purl=FALSE, include=FALSE}
library(knitr)
opts_chunk$set(dev=c("png", "pdf"))
opts_chunk$set(fig.height=6, fig.width=7, fig.path="Figures/", fig.alt="unlabelled")
opts_chunk$set(comment="", fig.align="center", tidy=TRUE)
options(knitr.kable.NA = '')
library(tidyverse)
library(broom)
library(tsibble)
library(fable)
library(feasts)
library(lubridate)
```


<!--- Do not edit anything above this line. --->
## Time-Indexed Regression: From Flexible Models to Trend

The previous lectures used the Seoul Bike data to show how model choice becomes harder when we have many plausible predictors, overlapping weather variables, and nonlinear relationships. From here we turn to a new kind of structure: observations recorded over time.

By the end of this lecture, students should be able to:

1. Explain why time-indexed data can need explicit time predictors.
2. Move from hourly observations to a daily aggregate for clearer time plots.
3. Build simple trend and seasonal predictors in an ordinary regression model.
4. Explain why time structure in the mean is separate from autocorrelation in the residuals.

`r xfun::embed_file("../Data/seoul_bike_daily.csv")`

```{r read seoul daily, message=FALSE}
seoul_daily = read_csv("../Data/seoul_bike_daily.csv")
seoul_daily
```

### Why Aggregate to Daily Data?

The hourly Seoul Bike data are useful for variable selection, collinearity, and nonlinear weather effects. For trend and seasonality, daily totals give a cleaner first view.

```{r daily-bike-plot, warning=FALSE, message=FALSE}
ggplot(seoul_daily, aes(x = day_index, y = rented_bike_count)) +
  geom_line() +
  labs(
    x = "Day index",
    y = "Daily rented bike count",
    title = "Daily bike demand has strong time structure"
  )
```

### Time as a Predictor

A first regression model can include a simple linear trend.

```{r daily-trend-model}
seoul_trend = lm(rented_bike_count ~ day_index, data = seoul_daily)
summary(seoul_trend)
```

This is still an ordinary linear regression. The difference is that one of the predictors records where the observation sits in time.

### Seasonal Predictors

The dataset also includes a coarse `season` variable.

```{r daily-season-model}
seoul_season = lm(rented_bike_count ~ day_index + season, data = seoul_daily)
summary(seoul_season)
```

This is the same regression idea as using any other factor: the mean response can differ between groups.

### Fourier Preview

Seasonal patterns can also be represented by sine and cosine terms.

```{r daily-fourier-preview}
seoul_daily = seoul_daily |>
  mutate(
    S1 = sin(2 * pi * day_index / 365),
    C1 = cos(2 * pi * day_index / 365)
  )

seoul_fourier = lm(
  rented_bike_count ~ day_index + S1 + C1 + mean_temperature + total_rainfall,
  data = seoul_daily
)
summary(seoul_fourier)
```

## Mean Structure First, Residual Dependence Later

Adding time predictors is about modelling the mean response:

$$E(Y_t) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 t + \beta_2 S_1(t) + \beta_3 C_1(t) + \cdots.$$

That is different from modelling residual dependence. A model can capture trend and seasonality in the mean and still have residuals that are correlated over time.

## Bridge to the Next Lecture

The next lecture develops these time predictors more fully: trend, season indicators, Fourier terms, and weather covariates in one regression model for daily Seoul Bike demand.
